content.body:
Water: H₂O
Properties:
• Universal solvent (polar molecule)
• Maximum density at 4°C
• Boiling point 100°C, freezing point 0°C
• High specific heat capacity
• Surface tension

Purification methods:
Sedimentation → Filtration → Chlorination
Distillation (for very pure water)

content.title: Water Purification
content.body:
Sedimentation: large particles settle out
Coagulation: alum (aluminium sulphate) added, fine particles clump
Filtration: sand/gravel filters remove smaller particles
Chlorination: chlorine kills bacteria (disinfection)
Fluoridation: prevents tooth decay (optional)
Distillation: boil, collect steam, condense — removes all dissolved substances

content.title: Tests for Water
content.body:
Test 1: Anhydrous copper(II) sulphate (white) + water → turns blue
Test 2: Anhydrous cobalt(II) chloride (blue) + water → turns pink/red
Boiling point: pure water boils at exactly 100°C at 1 atm
Freezing point: pure water freezes at exactly 0°C

questions.question: Chemical formula of water
questions.explanation: Water: two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom

questions.question: Anhydrous CuSO₄ turns blue when
questions.explanation: White anhydrous copper sulphate turns blue (hydrated CuSO₄·5H₂O) — test for water

questions.question: Chlorination of water serves to
questions.explanation: Chlorine is added to water supply to kill pathogens (bacteria, protozoa)

questions.question: Pure water boils at
questions.explanation: Pure water boils at exactly 100°C at standard atmospheric pressure

questions.question: Alum is added in water treatment to
options: make particles clump together (coagulation) for easier removal
questions.answer: make particles clump together (coagulation) for easier removal
questions.explanation: Aluminium sulphate (alum) causes colloidal particles to aggregate (flocculate) and settle

questions.question: Distillation produces the purest water because
options: boiling and condensing removes all dissolved substances
questions.answer: boiling and condensing removes all dissolved substances
questions.explanation: Distillation: only water evaporates (dissolved solids stay behind), condenses as pure water

questions.question: Cobalt chloride paper changes from blue to pink when
questions.explanation: Cobalt chloride: blue when dry, pink/red when moist (water present)

questions.question: Hard water contains
questions.explanation: Hardness: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions from dissolving limestone/dolomite prevent soap lathering

questions.question: Boiling removes temporary hardness because
options: calcium hydrogencarbonate decomposes: Ca(HCO₃)₂ → CaCO₃↓ + H₂O + CO₂
questions.answer: calcium hydrogencarbonate decomposes: Ca(HCO₃)₂ → CaCO₃↓ + H₂O + CO₂
questions.explanation: Heating decomposes calcium hydrogencarbonate to insoluble CaCO₃ (limescale) removing the hardness

questions.question: Permanent hardness is caused by
options: dissolved CaSO₄ and MgSO₄ (not removed by boiling)
questions.answer: dissolved CaSO₄ and MgSO₄ (not removed by boiling)
questions.explanation: Permanent hardness (CaSO₄, MgSO₄) is not removed by boiling — needs ion exchange or addition of washing soda

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Acids:
pH < 7, donate H⁺ ions in water
Strong: HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃ (fully ionised)
Weak: CH₃COOH, H₂CO₃ (partially ionised)

Bases:
pH > 7, accept H⁺ (or donate OH⁻)
Alkalis: bases soluble in water (NaOH, KOH, NH₃)

Neutralisation:
Acid + Base → Salt + Water
HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O

content.title: pH Scale
content.body:
pH 0-6: Acidic (stronger acid = lower pH)
pH 7: Neutral (pure water)
pH 8-14: Alkaline (stronger alkali = higher pH)

Indicators:
Litmus: red in acid, blue in alkali
Universal indicator: full colour range
Phenolphthalein: colourless (acid), pink (alkali)
Methyl orange: red (acid), yellow (alkali)

content.title: Making Salts
content.body:
Acid + Metal → Salt + Hydrogen
H₂SO₄ + Zn → ZnSO₄ + H₂

Acid + Metal Oxide → Salt + Water
2HCl + CuO → CuCl₂ + H₂O

Acid + Carbonate → Salt + Water + CO₂
2HCl + CaCO₃ → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂↑

Naming salts: HCl → chloride, H₂SO₄ → sulphate, HNO₃ → nitrate, H₃PO₄ → phosphate

questions.question: pH of a strong acid is
questions.explanation: Acids: pH < 7. Stronger acid = lower pH (more H⁺ ions)

questions.question: Neutralisation produces
questions.explanation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water (neutralisation reaction)

questions.question: Litmus paper turns red in
questions.explanation: Litmus: red in acid (pH<7), blue in alkali (pH>7), purple in neutral

questions.question: The salt formed from HCl reactions is a
questions.explanation: Hydrochloric acid (HCl) forms chloride salts

questions.question: Acid + carbonate produces
questions.explanation: Acid + carbonate → salt + water + CO₂ (carbon dioxide gas produced — test with limewater)

questions.question: An alkali is a base that
questions.explanation: Alkali = base that dissolves in water to form OH⁻ ions (e.g. NaOH, KOH, NH₃)

questions.question: Universal indicator is preferred over litmus because
options: it gives a range of colours indicating exact pH
questions.answer: it gives a range of colours indicating exact pH
questions.explanation: Universal indicator shows a spectrum of colours corresponding to pH values 1-14

questions.question: HNO₃ forms which salt?
questions.explanation: Nitric acid (HNO₃) forms nitrate salts

questions.question: H₂SO₄ + 2KOH →
questions.explanation: H₂SO₄ + 2KOH → K₂SO₄ + 2H₂O (sulphuric acid + potassium hydroxide)

questions.question: Phenolphthalein is colourless in acid and turns pink in
questions.explanation: Phenolphthalein: colourless in neutral/acid, bright pink/magenta in alkaline conditions

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Solution = solute + solvent
Concentration = amount of solute / volume of solution
g/L: mass concentration
mol/L (M): molar concentration
C = n/V  (C in mol/L, n in mol, V in litres)

Solubility: max mass dissolved in 100g water at given temperature
Usually increases with temperature for solids
Decreases with temperature for gases

content.title: Concentration Calculations
content.body:
Dissolve 5.85g NaCl (M=58.5 g/mol) in 200mL water
n = 5.85/58.5 = 0.1 mol
C = 0.1/0.2 = 0.5 mol/L

Dilution: C₁V₁ = C₂V₂
Dilute 50mL of 2M to 200mL:
C₂ = 2×50/200 = 0.5M

content.title: Saturated Solutions
content.body:
Saturated solution: maximum solute dissolved (equilibrium with undissolved solid)
Supersaturated: more than maximum (unstable)
Effect of temperature:
NaCl solubility: 35.7g/100g at 0°C, 39.2g at 100°C (slight increase)
KNO₃: 13.3g at 0°C, 246g at 100°C (steep increase)
CO₂ (gas): less soluble at higher T (why warm drinks go flat)

questions.question: Molar concentration formula
questions.explanation: Molarity C = moles of solute / volume of solution in litres

questions.question: 0.5 mol NaOH dissolved in 250mL. Concentration =
Dilution formula C₁V₁=C₂V₂. Dilute 100mL of 3M HCl to 300mL. New concentration =
Solubility of most solids in water
questions.explanation: Most solid solutes dissolve more at higher temperatures (endothermic dissolution process)

questions.question: Solubility of gases in water
questions.explanation: Gases less soluble at higher T: increased kinetic energy allows gas molecules to escape solution

questions.question: Saturated solution means
options: maximum solute dissolved at equilibrium with undissolved solid
questions.answer: maximum solute dissolved at equilibrium with undissolved solid
questions.explanation: At saturation: rate of dissolving = rate of crystallisation. Adding more solute: it just sits undissolved

questions.question: Moles of HCl in 250mL of 0.4M solution
Mass of solute from 2L of 0.5M NaCl (M=58.5g/mol)
Supersaturated solution is
options: more than maximum solute dissolved (unstable — crystallises easily)
questions.answer: more than maximum solute dissolved (unstable — crystallises easily)
questions.explanation: Supersaturated: obtained by careful cooling/evaporation. Unstable — seeding causes rapid crystallisation

questions.question: Why does carbonated drink go flat when warm?
options: Gas solubility decreases at higher temperature, CO₂ escapes
questions.answer: Gas solubility decreases at higher temperature, CO₂ escapes
questions.explanation: Higher temperature → CO₂ less soluble → bubbles out of solution → flat drink

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Rusting (iron corrosion):
4Fe + 3O₂ + 6H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃ → 2Fe₂O₃⋅3H₂O (rust)
Requires: oxygen AND water (both necessary)
Electrochemical process: Fe→Fe²⁺ at anode, O₂+H₂O→OH⁻ at cathode

Factors speeding corrosion:
Salt (electrolyte)
Acid (CO₂ dissolved in rain → carbonic acid)
Dissimilar metals in contact (galvanic corrosion)

content.title: Preventing Rusting
content.body:
Barrier methods: painting, oiling, greasing, plastic coating
Sacrificial protection: attach zinc to iron (zinc more reactive, oxidises first)
Galvanising: coating iron with zinc
Tin plating: tin less reactive — barrier only
Alloying: stainless steel (iron+chromium+nickel)
Electroplating: chromium, nickel, gold coatings
Cathodic protection: apply small negative voltage to iron structure

content.title: Reactivity and Corrosion
content.body:
More reactive metals corrode more readily
Reactivity series: K>Na>Ca>Mg>Al>Zn>Fe>Ni>Sn>Pb>Cu>Ag>Au
Al: highly reactive but forms protective Al₂O₃ layer (passivation) — resists further corrosion
Gold/platinum: noble metals, resist all corrosion

questions.question: Rusting requires both
questions.explanation: Classic demonstration: iron rusts where both O₂ and H₂O are present; dry air or boiled (oxygen-free) water alone don't cause rust

questions.question: Galvanising protects iron by
questions.explanation: Galvanising: hot-dip coating of iron/steel with zinc. Zinc provides both barrier and sacrificial protection

questions.question: Sacrificial protection works because
options: a more reactive metal (zinc/magnesium) corrodes preferentially, protecting the iron
questions.answer: a more reactive metal (zinc/magnesium) corrodes preferentially, protecting the iron
questions.explanation: Zinc (higher up reactivity series) oxidises before iron — even if iron is exposed, zinc corrodes to protect it

questions.question: Stainless steel resists corrosion due to
options: chromium content forming protective Cr₂O₃ oxide layer
questions.answer: chromium content forming protective Cr₂O₃ oxide layer
questions.explanation: Stainless steel (≥11% Cr): thin, stable Cr₂O₃ forms immediately on surface, blocking further oxidation

questions.question: Tin plating of food cans works as long as
questions.explanation: Tin less reactive than iron: barrier coating stops O₂/H₂O reaching iron. BUT if scratched, exposed iron corrodes faster (tin is cathodic)

questions.question: Salt speeds up rusting because
options: it is an electrolyte making the water more conductive, speeding electrochemical corrosion
questions.answer: it is an electrolyte making the water more conductive, speeding electrochemical corrosion
questions.explanation: Dissolved salt ions increase conductivity of water → electrochemical corrosion cells work more efficiently

questions.question: Aluminium objects are resistant to corrosion despite Al being reactive because
options: Al forms tenacious Al₂O₃ passivation layer
questions.answer: Al forms tenacious Al₂O₃ passivation layer
questions.explanation: Aluminium instantly forms a thin, stable Al₂O₃ layer which tightly adheres and prevents further oxidation

questions.question: Noble metals like gold resist corrosion because
options: they have very low reactivity and do not oxidise under normal conditions
questions.answer: they have very low reactivity and do not oxidise under normal conditions
questions.explanation: Au and Pt at bottom of reactivity series: high ionisation energy, do not react with O₂, H₂O, or most acids

questions.question: Cathodic protection of underground pipelines makes pipeline
questions.explanation: Applying negative voltage makes pipeline cathode: iron oxidation (Fe→Fe²⁺) cannot occur at cathode → no corrosion

questions.question: Rusting is described as an electrochemical process because it involves
options: oxidation at one site (anode) and reduction at another (cathode) with electron flow
questions.answer: oxidation at one site (anode) and reduction at another (cathode) with electron flow
questions.explanation: Rusting = electrochemical cell: Fe→Fe²⁺ (oxidation at anode), O₂→OH⁻ (reduction at cathode), electrons flow through metal

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Water. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Water concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Water builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Water. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Water. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Water include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Water. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
